help.translation: 'I had a great idea — if we collect powerful enough artefacts, we'll pull this off, won't we?'
"Luke... no matter how good the gear is, when the body inside is weak, when it's your time to die, you die."
Like images flashing through a dying man's lantern, a conversation with his old comrades raced through Cry's mind.
help.note: 走馬灯のように — Japanese idiom: "like a moving lantern," the slideshow of memories said to flash before someone in mortal danger.

help.translation: At a speed enough to launch even a mana-strengthened Hunter straight to heaven, his instincts had already given up on staying alive.
help.note: 天に召される is a literary euphemism — "be summoned to heaven," i.e. die. The narrator pairs it with the gruesome speed for ironic effect.

help.translation: ── I'm gonna die──!!
help.note: ッ marks an explosive cut-off / glottal stop in shouted speech.

tokens.s: うううううううううッ

help.translation: Diiiiiiiiieeeeee──!!
help.note: The drawn-out うううう mimics dragging-out terror.

help.translation: 【White Wolves' Den】 was wide enough that you couldn't believe it had ever been a beast's den.
It had decent width and height, but for someone flying about on a brake-less Black Wing of Night Sky, it was unbearably narrow.
help.note: 〜には + adjective + すぎる/た is the "too X for the purpose of Y" frame: あまりにも狭かった = "was far too narrow." 夜天の暗翼 is a treasure name (literally "the Dark Wing of Night Sky").

help.translation: It was dim inside the cave, but here and there, faintly glowing stones had been set.
Thanks in part to the Owl's Eye on his right thumb — an artefact granting night-vision — his field of view was perfectly serviceable.
A piece of luck inside a misfortune — but the misfortune was simply too big.
help.note: 不幸中の幸い is a stock idiom: "a fortunate piece in the middle of misfortune."

help.translation: A wall closing in.
Frantically working the artefact, he tore through the corner.
The cavern was dark and gloomy — a space he'd never voluntarily enter under normal circumstances; but right now there was room in his head for one thing only: how to stop.
help.note: いかにして〜か is a literary fixed pattern, "(the question of) how (one is to do X)."

help.translation: He'd brought a map, sure, but he no longer had any idea which corridor he was even in.
With this artefact's nonexistent agility, his body slammed into walls, ceiling, ground.
His vision shook violently with each impact.
He felt as if he'd been turned into a rubber ball or something.
He couldn't even tell what was happening anymore.
His face had been frozen in a grimace the whole time.
help.note: 〜っぱなし attaches to a verb stem to mean "left in (a state) the whole time / non-stop" — implying the state has gone on longer than expected, often with a tinge of helplessness.

help.translation: Calmly considered, he should have stopped before he ever entered the Treasury.
He'd gotten high on the absurd speed.
Going to puke.
Brought it on himself.
help.note: 自業自得 = "karmic results of one's own deeds" — "got what he asked for." The verbless ゲロ吐きそう and the noun-fragment style are characteristic of this narrator's slangy internal voice.

help.translation: He blew past the giant phantom that had been blocking the corridor.
Phantom or no — even one far above human limits couldn't catch him as he traced an erratic, bullet-fast path.
Reasonable, given that he himself didn't know what he was doing.
help.note: 〜とはいえ = "that said / be that as it may" — concessive, more literary than ても. 道理である = "stands to reason."

help.translation: By the time it noticed his approach and turned its head toward him, he was already directly above it.
He decided he hadn't seen the wolf walking on two legs holding a giant sword.
help.note: 見なかったことにする is a stock idiom: "to make it as if (one) didn't see (it)" — i.e. "pretend it never happened."

help.translation: ── Tino, where are you?!
Unlike phantoms, which vanish on death, a Hunter's body lingers a long time.
Even if she'd been eaten alive in a fight with a phantom, there'd have to be at least some trace left.
...Wouldn't there? Surely.
help.note: あり得ないんじゃないかなあ trails off — the なあ softens it into a hesitant self-question, characteristic of internal monologue.

help.translation: Cry's dynamic vision was pitiful, but at least nothing in the wildly streaking view looked like Tino's body — or those of her merry friends.
The chance she was dead was low.
After all this, if Tino's group were still hanging around the imperial capital and hadn't even left for the assignment, that'd be the punchline of the year.
help.note: お笑い種 = "the seed of a joke / laughingstock material."

help.translation: Tino takes responsibility seriously, unlike Cry, so she shouldn't have just tossed it aside — but as Liz's apprentice, she had a mischievous streak, so just maybe ──ggh!
help.note: 〜だけあって = "(precisely) because (she's) ~" — explains why someone fits a description; here, "as Liz's apprentice" justifies the tricky streak.

help.translation: He smashed the top of his head hard against the ceiling — no pain, but his vision shook again.
A long, straight corridor — a wolf-shaped phantom dead ahead saw the human missile that was Cry suddenly appear, and widened its eyes in shock.
But he blew past it.
His shoulder caught the side of its head; the recoil slammed him into the wall, jolting his entire body.
A sharp curve right after — body scraping the walls, he somehow rounded it.
That he hadn't, given his dynamic vision, already impaled himself on a wall was practically a miracle.
The other artefact assisting his trajectory was probably also helping a little.
Praise be to artefacts.
help.note: 宝具さまさま — さまさま is a slangy doubling of 様 used as an exclamation of gratitude ("thank goodness for ~").

help.translation: But — he was barely holding on, and one thing was clear: if he didn't fix this fast, he was going to die.
And he'd be remembered for all eternity as the idiot who, on a Black Wing of Night Sky already known to have killed people, became Human Missile #2 and crashed into a Treasury.
help.note: 未来永劫 = "for all eternity," set idiom. 〜と化す = "to transform into," literary.

help.translation: Really, really not having that.
That's just way too sad even for him.
He decided right there.
This is no good.
Whatever — once. Just stop, somehow.
If he kept this up, he'd snap.
At some point, he'd come out into a wider passage.
Right ahead, he could see the back of a giant phantom.
With the razor-sharp judgement of imminent death, he resolved to use it as a cushion.
All that was left was to brace himself.
Hugging his head, he shut his eyes and prayed for dear life.
And then — a shock more violent than anything before tore through his entire body.
And then — he came to.
His vision, sparking with shock, slowly steadied.
Apparently, he'd managed to stop in one piece.
He lowered the arms shielding his head and tried to stand — and realised he was already standing.
No damage.
The impact had been terrifying, but he'd made it out alive.
His semicircular canals were not okay.
With non-shaking ground under his feet for the first time in a while, he almost threw up — and somehow held it down.
He shook off the threat of blacking out and held on to consciousness.
Even an out-of-practice Hunter like him knew that losing consciousness in a Treasury meant death.
help.note: ブランクの長い〜 = "with a long absence (from active practice)."

help.translation: He brushed the dust off his shoulders and let out a long breath.
His heart was still screaming.
If he didn't calm it down quickly, it'd burst.
His face was still frozen stiff.
But considering he'd seen his life flash before his eyes, this much was a passing grade.
The Black Wing of Night Sky was a defective product — no question.
Whoever first invented it must've been every bit as cracked as his childhood friends.
help.note: イカれてる is slang, "cracked / off in the head." Compare with 狂ってる but more colloquial and less serious-sounding.

help.translation: How about — a brake function? As, like, the very first feature?
The phantom that had served as his cushion was buried head-first in the wall.
He hadn't seen well, but apparently there'd been two.
Stacked on top of each other, the fallen phantoms didn't twitch.
After taking a human missile to the back, even a Level-3 Treasury phantom was no match for it.
Their thick black armour was deeply caved in and cracked.
Lying near the wall was what looked like a giant bow and sword the phantom had been carrying.
These were nothing like the phantoms he'd been told about — different in size, shape, colour, everything. What were they?

The phantoms in this Treasury were supposed to be wolves, but the ones lying here wore thick armour even an upper-tier knight wouldn't bother with — different from the briefing in the worst possible way.
Long ago, when he'd been dragged to a Level-3 Treasury, the phantoms had been a bit... no, far weaker. Maybe this is just how they are now? It had been ages since he'd visited a Treasury.
Maybe they were all bark and no bite.
That was a possibility too — but he might still throw up.
Finally, he looked around.
His vision, blurred from a head-rush, sharpened.
Mid-flight he'd had no spare attention to track where he was — but this wasn't a corridor; it was a wide, open space.
The ceiling was higher than you'd expect underground; the floor and walls flatter than anything wolves would dig.
If only it had a window, was bright, wasn't damp, and didn't have phantoms — it'd be a great room.
And — he spotted someone he recognised.
Tousled black hair; pale cheeks.
A girl with no visible wounds, but who looked far more haggard than when he'd dumped the request on her at the clanhouse.
Tino, in other words.
The Tino he'd accidentally saddled with this strange assignment.
Gilbert and Master Greg's group were nearby too — all of them gasping for breath, staring at him in dumb astonishment.
Everyone, at least, was alive.
"Mas... ter?!"
"Tino, found you."
Lucky.
...No, no, 'found you' is not the right line.
He'd accidentally given her a too-casual greeting in his confusion — but this was a moment that called for a proper apology.
Even though she was unhurt, Tino's face was deathly pale, exhaustion bleeding through her usual composure to a degree that was hard to believe.
It was clear: this Level-3 Treasury had put a serious load on Tino.
Looks like the day to show his junior his world-class dogeza skill had finally come.
help.note: 土下座 = a formal full-prostration apology in which the apologiser kneels and presses their forehead to the ground; the most extreme apology gesture in Japanese culture, often used comedically here.

help.translation: Nothing left but to laugh.
As Cry smirked, Gilbert yelled with desperate intensity:
"H-hey, old man.
Behind, behind!"
"Eh?"
It's not 'old man.'
It's 'big brother.'
That this was the very first thing his head latched onto told you everything about how peace-rotted his out-of-practice brain had become.
Letting your guard down inside a Treasury — a disgrace to all Hunter-kind.
help.note: 〜の風上にも置けない is an idiom: literally "can't even be placed upwind of (X)," i.e. "a disgrace to (a profession or kind)."

help.translation: Turning casually around, what came into Cry's clueless field of view was a phantom of the same kind he'd just used as a cushion.
A single giant phantom in iron-black armour.
Its sheer presence sent his chicken body drifting toward the wall on its own.
Looking more carefully, he saw another one near Gilbert too.
This one carrying a club so large it was almost a joke.
Two visible — plus the two on the ground, four in total.
He hadn't noticed at the time of impact, but their heads weren't human at all — they were wolves'.
And the right half of those heads was covered by a human skull.
Through the gloom, dully glinting eyes red as blood looked down on the rude intruder who'd just crashed in.
Their shoulders heaved with rough breathing.
From their jaws, looking ready to split open, drool dripped in viscous strings.
The usual Cry would have collapsed and thrown up just at that gaze.
But the peace-rotted, sense-numbed Cry of the moment had a different reaction.
help.note: 平和ボケ = idiom, "peace-rotted / softened by peace" — a person who's lost their edge from too long without danger.

help.translation: Huh — so this is what shows up at Level 3 these days.
Progress.
If a Level 3 is this, what would Level 8 even spit out.
Glad I quit Treasury work.
Past me — masterful judgement.
A god, maybe?
The wolf with the heavy gun let out a low growl at the smirking Cry and stepped back.
The wolf with an iron rod tall enough to brush the ceiling, standing in front of Gilbert, shifted as if to guard him.
Its nose worked.
Its sharp eyes narrowed; it was watching him carefully.
Only then did Cry finally grasp the situation, and let his smile drop.
Wait.
This — am I actually dying here?
A pinch?
They aren't attacking, but a promising Hunter like Tino got chewed up by them — there's no way I beat them.
What now.
As Cry frantically searched for a way out, Master Greg shouted in a voice that trembled:
"N-no way... that... that boss... is afraid?!"
"Afraid?"
Ridiculous.
If they're wolves, I'm a sheep.
A sheep whose mana-strength has mostly worn off, just one with a high certification level.
In front of a Cry who hadn't even fully processed the situation, however, the wolf-knight took another step back.
Its nose pumped up and down; its attention had peeled off Tino's group entirely and was on him alone.
Sure enough — there was strong wariness in its eyes.
What about him, exactly, was scary?
Master Greg's scarier.
He followed its line of sight.
He realised what it was looking at.
The blood-red gaze wasn't aimed at his face but at his chest — at the metal capsule on the chain around his neck, the one that was supposed to contain Citry slime.
He stepped forward.
The wolf-knight stepped back.
Its eyes were on him, but not really seeing him.
Hmmm?
Oh-hoh?
If a phantom this big is scared just from seeing the capsule — what on earth is in there?
What had he brought with him?
When he closed the distance another step, the wolf-knights all moved two steps back together.
...They'd completely decided he was a poisoned sheep.
His luck was turning.
Apparently this wasn't where he was going to die.
Without taking his eyes off the wolf-knights, he tossed words behind him.
help.note: 〜ことなく = "without (doing) ~," a literary alternative to 〜ないで. Reads as more polished and reserved than the colloquial form.

help.translation: He kept up a calm front, but his heart was still pounding hard enough to hurt.
"Tino, can you run?"
"Y-yes... of course!"
Tino, who'd been frozen, gave him a bright reply at his words.
There were three corridors out of this room.
The one straight ahead was blocked by wolf-knights.
Even with them frightened, he couldn't rule out them eventually concluding 'a poisoned snack is still a snack.'
Punching past two of those giants was impossible.
The play was to fall back for now, let Tino's group rest, and then break out together.
"That way."
He pointed at the nearest right-hand corridor.
It was the one the iron-rod wolf had been blocking until a moment ago.
It had moved, so they may as well take the gift.
"Um, Master.
Wouldn't it be better to take them down?"
Tino asked, looking apologetic.
Mm-hm. Yeah.
Sure, if you can take them down, you should.
Like I'd ever take them down — are you screwing with me?
help.note: 舐めてんのか is slang: 舐める ("to lick / make light of") + てる + のか — "are you making fun of me?"

help.translation: Throwing the Citry slime at them and praying it killed them was an option, but trusting his fate to a slime he didn't even know the properties of was too risky.
If it worked even sealed inside the capsule, may as well leave it that way.
He sighed and lectured his cute junior gently:
"Tino, you mustn't lose sight of what's important."
"!! That, is──"
The most important thing.
Goes without saying.
It is — your own life.
Battles where you stake your life look ridiculous from where I sit.
It's their funeral if they want — but I will not be doing that.
Just then, somewhere, there came a clatter.
Tino let out a small cry.
A shadow crossed his vision.
Black armour was bearing down on him.
The wolf-knight he'd taken out earlier with his human-missile attack had revived and closed the distance in one stride.
By the time he realised it, a blade as long as his own height was already swinging down at him from directly above.
A roar charged with fury and pressure, plus animal stench, rocked all his senses.
His body locked up as if its sinews had snapped taut.
He couldn't react.
Couldn't even move a finger.
Like a guillotine, the blade fell from above.
A blow strong enough to easily split him lengthwise like a green bamboo crashed down on him,
── and was deflected without leaving a single scratch.
"...What?"
Master Greg's voice.
The closing wolf-knight's eyes flew wide.
It clearly hadn't seen that coming.
It backed up several paces and, just for that one moment, forgot its rage and stared down at the sword in its hands.
Right after, with a gunshot's roar, a giant arrow took him in the forehead — and was deflected just as easily.
Apparently not one of the phantoms he'd plowed into had actually died.
And — they were furious.
Naturally.
If he got slammed into a wall from behind out of nowhere, he'd be furious too.
Bow, greatsword, and the other two wolf-knights — they all glared at him.
All Cry could do was force a wry smile.
That was all he could do.
This is — death.
He was going to die.
It was at that point that he finally remembered to do something offensive in return.
He raised his index finger and pointed it at the wolf-knights like a gun.

tokens.r: かけるしょうげきのゆびわ

help.translation: On his left pinky was an artefact — one of the Snap Rings, the Ring of Charging Impact — and he activated it.
help.note: 弾指 ("snap-finger") is the umbrella term in this work for ring-shaped artefacts that fire magical bullets — read as such throughout.

help.translation: A blue light kindled at his fingertip and condensed into a magical bullet.
Just before he loosed it, a hard-boiled line popped into his head, and pure spinal reflex made him say it out loud.
"Sorry to disappoint you.
My lives — there's seventeen of 'em."
Treasure Hunting is a world of talent.
Humans are weak.
In particular, their physical capacity ranks near the bottom among the countless living things, ill-suited to braving harsh Treasuries or fighting monsters and phantoms.
To break out of that rule and make it as a Hunter, certain talents — beginning with mana-material absorption rate — are absolutely required.
Even in this Treasure-Hunter-fawning era, this is exactly why the number of Hunters never rises beyond a certain ceiling.
What was unfortunate for Cry was that he only realised this after becoming a Hunter.
And what was lucky was that, among his childhood friends, he was the only one without that talent.
The party they'd named 《Lamenting Spectres》 had enough talent — even with him pulled out — to clear Treasuries with ease.
And the wealth they brought back, plus the reputation they piled up, made Cry just slightly more 'tolerable.'
So even though he had no talent, no courage, no drive — no dreams, no hopes, no luck — he was still alive.
The Wardspan ranks among ring-type artefacts as one of the best-known, alongside the Snap Rings.
Its effect: when struck, it automatically projects a barrier of fixed strength for a fixed time.
To put it plainly, stripping the fancy language: it blocks one attack.
Even within Wardspans, types vary by barrier strength and duration.
Prices and rarity differ accordingly, but Cry — utterly unwilling to die under any circumstances — bought up the most expensive ones he could lay hands on, regardless of strength or duration.
Number now in his possession — seventeen.
Enough money to buy two or three clan headquarters.
A man who routinely wears that many Wardspans — items a top-tier Hunter would carry as a single emergency charm — is something the imperial capital probably has only one of.
Naturally, with only ten fingers he couldn't wear all of them, so the rest were stashed in a pouch — but the effect carried across all the same.
In other words, without these, no way could he use a horror like the Black Wing of Night Sky.
Of course — they weren't invincible.
A Wardspan's barrier lasts at most a second.
Usually it's instantaneous.

Once it triggers, all the magic charged into it is gone, and it becomes an ordinary ring; he'd already burned through some during the wall-collisions on the way in, so a few more solid hits and he'd be paste.
He had to escape before that happened, no matter what.
'Seventeen lives' was a complete overstatement.
"Hh... it's dodging it."
Ruda yelled.
The wolf-knight with the greatsword reacted with extreme speed to the arrow-fast blue bullet.
Aimed at the head, it dodged with a slight crouch.
Cry frowned at that movement.
It moved as if it knew the bullet was coming.
The blue bullet whisked past overhead — and a dull thud shook the cavern.
The wolf-knight was slammed to the ground by an intense impact.
Just past it, the bullet had reversed course mid-air and struck it in the back of the head.
The wolf-knights wavered.
Cry didn't take his eyes off them as he yelled:
"Tino, run!"
"!? Y-yes!"
Tino and Gilbert's group jolted into a sprint.
The wolf-knights were watching only Cry; they didn't follow.
'Snap Ring' is the umbrella term for ring-shaped artefacts that fire magical bullets.

tokens.r: かけるしょうげきのゆびわ

help.translation: The Ring of Charging Impact is a Snap Ring that — at full charge — can fire up to seven bullets that deliver a strong impact on contact.
But — for all the flash, the actual power is almost nothing.
The wolf-knight that took it to the head was probably just slightly startled.
Snap Rings come in many varieties, but none are powerful enough to actually take down a phantom — usable only for keeping one in check, at best.
The downed wolf-knight pushed itself up slowly, hands on the ground.
As Cry expected — no visible wounds.
The wolves arranged themselves around him in a fan.
Two front, two back. Decent balance.
Cry watched them, and only now noticed the firearm one of the rear wolves was holding, and frowned.
That gun is bad news.
Rapid-fire is the worst possible match for me, isn't it?
The Citry-slime intimidation was wearing off — the counterattack had pushed anger to the front.
In their eyes: about 10% fear, 30% anger, 30% hate, 30% caution. (Made-up figures.)
First priority — buy time for Tino to escape.
If it's just him, worst case he can fly out again.
Holding a weapon would at least slow them down somewhat.
Forcing a smirk, he reached behind for the artefact sword in its sheath — and his hand caught nothing.

tokens.r: ぜったいだんがんあてるくん

help.translation: He kept grabbing, but all he touched were the crossbow-shaped artefact — the trajectory-controller — and the so-called 'Definitely Hits Bullets-kun' that had bent the path of the magic bullets earlier (named by him; despite the name, no actual bullet-hitting power).
Are you kidding... did I drop it?
Sheath there. Contents not.
He thought back over the run, but he'd been too busy not getting battered to remember when.
And it had been so expensive.
Well — either way, it didn't have any ability that would have changed this situation...
The wolf-knights eyed the man going through nonsensical motions warily.
"Master?! What are you──"
Tino, who was supposed to have run, was still watching him from the corridor mouth.
Not just Tino — the others were waiting too.
I told you to run, godddamit.
And as for what he was doing — he wanted to know himself.
What had he just been trying to do.
Dropping artefacts inside a Treasury — what is he even doing?
Forget bad luck.
Just plain stupid.
...Just plain stupid.
The phantom hugging its club with both hands roared as if forcing itself past its own fear, and stepped forward.
On his right pinky was the Mouseling's Wisdom artefact, which sensed danger; it picked up the killing intent and warmed up.
Of course there was no way to dodge.
A heavy arc of the club came down to flatten his entire body — and was deflected by the barrier.
Bad.
Worse than expected — there's no fixing this.
Body wouldn't move.
Even knowing he was safe, it was scary.

tokens.r: ちょうじゅうりょうきゅう

help.translation: The phantoms stared at the Cry who hadn't budged an inch under that ultra-heavy strike, themselves rattled.
Cry was rattled too.

tokens.r: ちゅうじつないぬのくさり

help.translation: The Faithful Hound's Chain at his belt rattled as if alarmed at its master's danger.
It was a valuable item, and he'd hate to see it broken — but life over treasure.
It should at least slow them down.
With a click, he popped the chain off his belt.
The Hound's Chain, freshly recharged, leapt up as if it had been waiting and snake-streaked toward the wolf-knight with the club.
It didn't have killing power, but for a giant phantom it would be exquisitely annoying.
It coiled around the wolf's leg and broke its balance.
Apparently the Hound's Chain wasn't something the other three had encountered — they were on guard.
Mm-hm. Scary, isn't it.
First time he saw it, it freaked him out too.
But one Hound's Chain wasn't going to keep them off forever.
Plus — gun and bow, terrifying.
This world is full of awful stuff.
If he just bolted, would they chase him?
The wolf-knights were on guard against him — well, against the Citry slime, more than him.
Yet undeniable rage was in their eyes that they couldn't bottle up.
I just want to escape.
The hell with rescue targets — I want to go home.
Cry, fed up, opened both hands.
And activated his Snap Rings.
Among ring-type artefacts, the better-known ones split into two camps — those that work only when worn, and those that work just from being held; few people know about that distinction.
And — Snap Rings are the latter.
Gilbert went slack-jawed.
On Cry's palms hovered countless bullets of light.
He'd activated every Snap Ring he'd crammed into his belt-pouch.
One advantage of ring-type artefacts: they're light.
And Snap Rings are quite cheap, as artefacts go.
So with a bit of money and a bit of effort, you can do something like this.
The colour of the magic bullets varies by Snap Ring type.
The countless bullets bobbing in the air were colourful, eye-catching, and showy. (Also, very weak.)
The wolf-knights stirred.
Visually it looked like an ordinary magic attack, so they were probably on guard.
But it was pointless.
Dodging this many bullets was impossible.

tokens.r: ぜったいだんがんあてるくん

help.translation: Magic bullets travel in straight lines unless fired from a special Snap Ring — but Cry has 'Definitely Hits Bullets-kun.'
The bullets floated up and, on his command, traced wild three-dimensional paths in to attack the wolf-knights.
The wolf-knights scattered to dodge — but he just kept shifting their trajectories to corner them.
No room even to swing a weapon.
Apparently deciding there was no dodging — or maybe mistaking it for full-tracking — the wolf-knights dropped to the ground.
He poured magic bullets onto the wolf-knights cowering like turtles.
"S-so cool..."
"This is... Level 8──"
Gilbert's voice carried admiration, as if for a circus act.
Ruda and Master Greg seemed to share the view.
Even Tino's eyes were sparkling.
Honest praise was nice.
A tip would be nicer, but that aside — please, just go already.
Bullets struck home.
On heads, on arms, on shoulders, on eyes, on the skulls they wore.
Like flame burning, like water freezing, like a paralysing jolt, like a bursting spark — different bullets made different sounds.
No two of the Snap Rings he'd brought were the same.
The phantoms let out a low, animal-like groan.
The light of the magic bullets faded, and the dark returned.
By the way — Cry's tactic was flashy and very impressive-looking, but it had one fatal flaw.
............it was very weak.
Under everyone's breath-held watch, the wolf-knights — who had endured the attack like turtles — got up as if nothing had happened.
Not a scratch on body or head.
"!? B-but you hit them, so many times──"
Ruda's voice was on the verge of tears.
Not just Ruda — even the phantoms that had taken the attack were letting out vaguely puzzled growls.
Couldn't be helped.
Most artefacts aren't designed to kill.
Weapon-shaped artefacts are a separate matter, but those depend a lot on the user's ability — useless to a man with zero martial talent.
The phantoms got up, confirmed they were unharmed, and all glared together at the man who'd loosed that play-attack on them.
Doesn't work, huh... Among Snap Rings there are also paralysis types, sleep types, and so on.
He'd hoped maybe one would land — apparently they were nullified.
Well — they're meant for use against people, so against monsters, can't be helped.
And with that — his options were almost gone.
No choice — he had to commit.
If he held this line, it'd be a war of attrition.
"Well, well — guess there's no other way. I didn't want to use this, but──"
Half-resigned, he took the metal capsule from his neck — about the size of an index finger.
The wolf-knights' eyes widened, and as if remembering, they all stepped back several paces.
Yeah — what they're scared of is this, not me.
Knew that already.
If he was going to die anyway, all of them might as well be devoured by this Citry slime — made by his childhood friend Citry-chan, an absurdly dangerous, slime?
(He didn't know exactly what, and didn't want to know.)
Trembling fingers undid the cap; before throwing, he peered carefully into the capsule.
He rubbed his eyes and checked again.
Frowned.
Tino's group was watching him, worried.
He nodded once and put the cap back on.
He wound up and threw it at the wolf-knights — and at the same time fired a shockwave bullet at it.
The wolf-knights stirred and quickly opened up the distance.
The instant he saw the trajectory-controlled magic bullet hit the capsule, he sprinted toward Tino.
"Tino, hurry!"
At Cry's onrush, Tino's group snapped out of it and all began running.
The metal capsule popped.
The wolf-knights roared as if to threaten — but no time to bother with them.
He had to get away before they realised it was empty.
help.note: 〜ないうちに = "before (X happens)" — uses 内 ("the time-window during which X has not yet happened") rather than 前に.

help.translation: ...What is that thing?
Where did the contents go?
Scary.
He ran all-out as if squeezing every last bit of strength.
Forcing his breath even as he ran, just moving his legs.
It had been a long time since he'd run.
No room left to worry about what was behind him.
He just kept pushing through the dim, narrow corridor.
Cold air brushed his cheek.
Master Greg, Gilbert, Ruda, and Tino were running just ahead of him.
He was at full effort, but the gap wasn't closing.
He realised something.
Wait — what?
Are they slowing down so they don't leave me behind?
Gilbert, who was casually keeping pace despite carrying his greatsword, glanced back at Cry and frowned.
Despite the dire straits he'd been in before Cry showed up, his expression now had room to spare.
Did he heal up while running, somehow.
"At that pace, they'll catch up.
Push the speed more──"
"Idiot!
Cry's keeping pace with the injured Tino!"
"!! O-oh — sorry."
At Ruda's bark, Gilbert apologised, snapping to attention.
Eh? Tino was injured?
And full-speed me──
And my full-speed pace is the same as injured Tino's?
Eh? My speed isn't slow, right?
Tino's just fast, right?
Wait — am I subconsciously slowing down for her?
It stung a little — but the comment had also calmed him down.
He confirmed there was no strange sound behind, and stopped.
He didn't have any thief skills, but if there were pursuers, Tino would tell him.
Looked like they'd shaken them.
Seeing him stop, the others stopped too.
They'd warmed up to him during the dungeon — pretty obedient.
"??? It's okay now?"
"Looks like we shook 'em.
That was bad.
Really, you saved us."
Master Greg was thanking him — but really the one who should be apologising was Cry.
But for now, regrouping was the priority.
Suppressing the urge to vomit, he caught his breath and looked at Tino.
Tino, meeting his gaze, hugged her own shoulders as if frightened.
"Mas-Master..."
"Cry.
Tino — sh-she really tried hard.
Without her, before you came to save us, we'd definitely have been wiped out──"
Ruda spoke as if making excuses.
"Yes, yes.
I know.
...Sorry.
If 'sorry' was enough, what a wonder that would be."
He didn't need to be told.
Looking at her, you could see she'd worked hard.
She must have been moving violently — her usually neat hair was a mess; her face white as if she'd lost all blood.
Something must have caught the right thigh of her black shorts: it was torn, exposing pale skin, and the contrast pulled his eyes in despite himself.
...She's grown up.
help.note: 色っぽくなっちゃって — 〜ちゃって (= 〜てしまって) here adds the resigned/affectionate "despite herself" nuance: "she's gone and gotten alluring on me."

help.translation: Noticing his gaze, Tino abruptly grabbed her hem and pulled it up, baring more of her thigh.
...What are you doing in a place like this, you.
The hem's already short — your underwear is going to show.
Tino looked away, embarrassed.
As Cry stared at the lip-pressed Tino, Gilbert spoke up, exasperated:
"《Thousand Trials》, you can do healing too?"
..............oh — that's where she was hurt?
You should have said.
I didn't notice.
I thought it was the usual mischief.
Well — actually, the reason he'd stopped was for treatment after all.
She offered up the developed pale thigh as if displaying it.
Faint blood-vessels visible under the skin, but no wound.
Well — invisible damage, then.
Apparently bad enough that she could only run as fast as full-speed me.
Of course he had healing artefacts.
Of course he did — there was no way he wouldn't.
He took the silver crucifix necklace from his neck — Devotion of the Compassionate — and pressed it to Tino's thigh.
Blue light flowed from the crucifix, soaking into her thigh, and her expression eased for a moment.
Sorry — for not noticing.
"Thank you.
Master.
The pain is gone."
Well — he still needed her to do plenty more, after all.
Watching the healing, Gilbert said, sounding relieved:
"Ah — even the healing is an artefact, of course."
All? It's all artefacts — what about it?? Hm?
Got a problem?
If this weren't a Treasury, he'd have lost his temper and gone home.
"Cry.
Those wolf-knights — did you defeat them?"
Master Greg asked, watching the corridor they'd just come down.
Defeated? He almost certainly hadn't.
Wolves have a sharp sense of smell.
Those wolf-knights, or whatever they were?
What they had been afraid of was the Citry-slime smell on the capsule.
Whether slimes have a smell, he didn't know — but it had to be that, or nothing else fit.
By now they were probably furious.
Tricked by an empty metal capsule, prey gone.
What they had to focus on now was just running.
They'd looked horrible, but as long as they cleared the Treasury, those phantoms wouldn't follow.
After all, the rescue targets are surely dead.
Dying yourself trying to save dead people is the height of pointless.
He sighed and stretched up.
Losing the sword was a real shame, but life over property.
He couldn't be sure even the Hound's Chain would come back; he'd have someone retrieve it once they got back to the clan.
"Didn't beat 'em.
But at the time, that was... the best option.
The thing to think about now isn't them.
Let's keep walking."
"O-okay."
Now then — the question was: where was 'here,' and where was the way out?
Cry led them and they walked in silence.
Out of fatigue, no one talked.
Per the map he'd checked beforehand, 【White Wolves' Den】 was a structure where narrow corridors branched like an ant-nest.
Translation: the scenery looked the same everywhere, and you couldn't tell which part of which corridor you were in.
It wasn't an especially big Treasury, but it was possible they were retracing their own path.
Wait — why am I in front?
I'm not a thief, am I?
Isn't this a thief job?
Why are there even two thieves in this party.
He'd tried stopping to make them go ahead — but whenever he stopped, Tino's group also stopped, so he kept ending up in front no matter what.
Where's that initiative of yours, Tino-san?
When he looked at her, their eyes met, and she immediately looked away.
It was as if she was rejecting him.
As if she was saying 'I don't want to talk to my master, please drop dead.'
To be looked at like that by Tino, of all people, was a shock.
Hm? Should I have just dogeza'd already?
In this dangerous Treasury?
Game over?
Nothing for it but to walk forward in confusion.
Sometimes he turned into a side passage on a whim.
Maybe this Treasury just didn't have many phantoms — they hadn't run into a single one, the only saving grace.
Probably Tino was quietly steering them away from the dangerous spots.
Now and then, a roar echoed through the tunnels — but it was still distant.
Distant, he thought.
He hoped it was distant.
He really hoped.
But — they'd walked quite a while and weren't reaching the exit.
Direction-wise he was probably right, but this was exactly why cavern Treasuries were the worst.
Time to dogeza Tino, perhaps.
While Cry was thinking it over, Gilbert finally couldn't take it anymore:
"Hey... if you've got a reason for not telling us, sorry, but... where are we headed?
The exit?"
He'd grown awfully meek...
But unfortunately, Cry himself didn't know either.
Just as he was about to say so, Tino cut in:
"Gilbert — reading Master's intent is part of the training.
And — we are not heading to the exit.
From the boss room, the right-hand corridors don't connect to the exit, no matter where you go.
To reach the exit, you have to pass through the boss room."
"S-seriously... in this situation, training?"
S-seriously...
Internally, he had the same reaction as Gilbert.
We're going to the exit.
At least, the place I'm trying to reach is, without a doubt, the exit.
But oh — the right-hand corridors don't connect, huh.
...Wait, that was a boss room?
Made sense, then, that the phantoms there were stronger than expected.
What is this, then?
We have to go back??
And — what, Tino?
You were training in this situation?
If not the exit, where do you think we're heading?
Did I have something else in mind, perhaps?
This is what comes of being too hard on yourself...
"But — uh, Cry.
Could you tell us where we're heading, already──"
Ruda asked, carefully.
Her voice made him feel pathetic.
Where am I going?
I'm always lost in life.
No signpost.
If anything, somehow he had become the signpost himself.
Anyway — let's casually U-turn.
Plenty of time has passed; surely those guys are gone.
Ah — I'd like to U-turn my life too.
He almost cried; he firmed up his expression instead.
He picked a turn.
Two turns the same way equals a U-turn, effectively.
...Is this really going to be okay?
After several minutes of regaining his pace, just as he was thinking about making the second turn, Master Greg spoke in a hoarse voice.
Cry turned and found Greg looking at him as if at a monster.
"Impossible... There were no traces...
You weren't even looking — how──"
"...That's what I told you.
Master doesn't do anything random."
"This isn't the time for that!
W-we have to help them!"
Ruda took off.
Only then did Cry finally notice the figures lying in the corridor far ahead.
Too small to be phantoms.
Squinting, he could see they were faintly moving.
What is that?
Master Greg, you noticed that just now and pieced it together?
You all really do have great eyes.
I might've turned without noticing them.
But — could it be... the rescue targets?
Still alive, somehow.
Lucky.
Tino puffed out her chest with what could only be called pride and looked at him as if dazzled.
"That's what I said.
Master is reading everything."
"Come on — common-sense-wise, this had to be a coincidence."
There's no way to read the future like that — not even an artefact could.
"...Why are you of all people saying that, when you're the one who led us here."
Gilbert gave him a long-suffering look at this obvious-truth declaration.
The rescue target was a man even bigger than Master Greg.
Full plate armour gleaming a dull silver, a large green-painted shield.
Beside him, an enormous conical lance — never used in human-vs-human warfare — placed where he could grab it instantly.
From its glow, almost certainly an artefact.
Rodolphe Davout.
A name Cry hadn't heard before, and the man was sprawled out at the moment, but his enormous frame had the kind of presence one would believe of a Level-5-certified Hunter.
Apparently Tino and Greg knew him.
...You guys took my request knowing a Level-5 Hunter was missing?
I'm out.
Apparently bones were broken; they rushed to him, and with smooth movements Cry couldn't have followed for a second, stripped off the armour and got him drinking a potion.
Tino's group did, that is.
help.note: Same stylistic move as p444 / p64 — the narrator describes an action neutrally and then tags the actual subject as an afterthought.

help.translation: Other members lying around were also battered and several were in serious condition, but apparently everyone was just barely alive.
That they hadn't been finished off, lying down in the depths of a Treasury like this, was practically a miracle.
"Pain?"
Rodolphe rasped his thanks.
His cheeks were sunken, but the back of his eyes still held the spark of life that hadn't quite gone out.
"Hh... haa, haa...
I-I'm okay. Th-thanks.
Y-you saved us."
"Thank Master."
"Hold on, I didn't really do anything──"

tokens.r: しょうしんしょうめい

help.translation: Genuinely, he hadn't done anything useful.
About all he'd done was send Tino in.
...Wait.
Should I be the one being thanked?
Rodolphe looked up at him, eyes hazy.
Three days in a hole — even with the pain gone, the exhaustion would be severe.
So at least he handed over a chocolate bar he kept as a snack.
Rodolphe gnawed at it as if devouring it.
Cry waited until he was done, then asked:
"Food?"
"Hh... ngh... O-out side."
"Master, ours is outside too.
We were planning to camp outside."
"Ah — so it is.
We always camped inside."
His childhood friends had a tendency to view dangerous Treasuries as convenient training grounds.
After things settled, he checked the situation again.
A few were still unconscious, but with potions in them, no immediate risk of death.
But — the fact that all of them were alive created a brand-new problem.
From the Explorers' Association's view it was great news; from the rescuers' view it couldn't be more inconvenient.
First, hauling five injured people out is a big job.
With phantoms like those around, even more so.
Cry's group didn't have any margin either.
Rodolphe was a Level-5 Hunter and seemed reliable, but starved and dehydrated for three days, fighting that phantom would be impossible.
And after all — losing was why he was here in the first place.
Could he even move?
In armour that big.
Carrying him is just impossible.
He probably couldn't even lift the lance.
If only Cry hadn't dropped his sword on the way; that might have changed things.
Worst case, they'd have to take the armour off.
Hanging around here, no telling when phantoms would arrive.
Rodolphe might be lucky — but Cry was spectacularly unlucky.
Gazing into Rodolphe's still-unfocused eyes, the situation-check began.
Tino did, that is.
help.note: ティノが at the very end is a hallmark stylistic move of this narrator — he describes an action neutrally, then adds the actual subject as an afterthought to highlight that he himself wasn't doing it.

help.translation: The matter demanded swift action.
"What happened?
You're Level 5... you should easily be able to fight in this Treasury."
Right.
Level 5 is what you'd call top-tier.
Not the kind of pitiful Hunter whose level alone got bumped up, like Cry.
And Rodolphe didn't enter this Treasury alone, either.
At Tino's question, Rodolphe pressed his lips together for a moment, then began to speak in a trembling voice.
His wide green eyes communicated, by themselves, the size of the fear he had felt.
"Haa, haa... s-something dangerous, in there.
This isn't Level 3 — there's something serious in here.
I wasn't off-guard.
But — it didn't reach.
It didn't land.
The lance, this thing's attacks too──"
"Yeah, we get it.
The wolf-knight that hides the right half of its face with human bone, right?
We fought 'em."
Gilbert gave a shrug at the trembling Rodolphe's words.
Reading the room: not his strong suit.
But Rodolphe shook his head, eyes wide:
"H-half?
N-no.
The one that — got us — that wounded us — was a phantom hiding its entire face in bone.
Run, hurry──"
Pale as a sheet.
The wide-open eyes shook in terror as if seeing 'that enemy' even now.
At his words, Tino's expression hardened, and she looked at Cry.
Don't look at me, it's not my fault...
But — there's something stronger than that?
What is going on in this Treasury.
...However unlucky he was — surely they wouldn't actually run into it... right?
He wanted to laugh it off but couldn't get there.
Master...
Amazing.
God! God!
"...How is that working?"
"Secret."
As Tino watched her admired Hunter pull chocolate bars from a small leather pouch one after another and hand them out to his comrades, her chest filled with feeling.
The imperial capital is wide.
There are plenty of excellent Hunters, but if she had to pick one, Tino would pick Cry Andrich.
His brave figure — friendly side included — was the very thing she looked up to.
"You don't have anything but chocolate?"
"Nope.
But there's plenty of chocolate bars."
Ruda's exasperated face, Gilbert's blunt remark — neither bothered him.
The chocolate bars coming out of the small leather pouch as if by magic loosened the tense atmosphere.
Tino's master, 'Big Sister,' is strong.
Frighteningly strong — but Master is not just strong.
The kindness to come and rescue, at the last possible moment, an apprentice who couldn't overcome a trial.

Even mid-battle — when phantoms like those he could surely have crushed in a moment — even saving the time that would have taken: the judgement to prioritise rescuing the people who had been forgotten about (slipped from her own mind).
The tracking ability, putting any thief to shame, that walked them right to the missing party.
That no phantoms had appeared on the way must have been because he could sense them — or, like in the boss room, the phantoms had feared his presence.
And — not to be overlooked — the strength to cast aside his pride and play the fool to lighten the mood.
When people talk about the strongest of the young Hunters, Arc's name comes up most — but Arc could not do that.
help.note: 〜まい is a literary negative-volitional / negative-conjecture form ("surely will not / cannot ~"), bookish and often used to express the speaker's strong personal judgement.

help.translation: All of it, perfect.
From Tino's view, no one was more deserving of Level 10 than Master.
His habit of giving the people around him grueling trials calibrated to his own talent was a problem — but even that was a kind of love (the rod).
When things got truly impossible, he'd come and rescue you, like this.
You couldn't really call that a flaw.
"What now?"
"Of course — getting out of here as fast as possible.
Mission accomplished."
At Greg's question, Master answered immediately.
For Hunters, fighting and beating a strong enemy is an honour.
But there was no hesitation in his answer.
He must be thinking of the rescued people's stamina.
Even after high-calorie chocolate bars, the six were nowhere near full strength.
Or maybe — phantoms like those weren't even worth Master's time anyway...
Just looking at him put strength back into Tino's exhausted body.
She didn't want her admired hero to see her looking pathetic any longer.
It was bad enough she'd failed to clear the trial.
She'd been overjoyed when he came to save her — but she also wanted his approval.
Even if Tino's strength was, viewed from Level 8, no more than dust.
Then Master's eyes turned to her.
Just that made her heart leap.
And admired Master smiled gently and said:
"Well — the leader of this assignment is Tino, so let's go with what Tino says."
"!? Eh... but... me? Compared to Master, I'm──"
Her real feelings slipped out.
No — anyone in her place would feel the same.
Hardly any Hunter in the imperial capital could match the undefeated 《Thousand Trials》 — Level 8, deeply experienced, capable of reading a Treasury's anomalies from a city desk.
But to the cowering Tino, Master said with a serious face:
"This is experience too.
If push comes to shove, I'll help you."
Once told that, she couldn't say 'leave it all to Master.'
Tino kept glancing at his expression as she thought it through her own way and answered.
"I — I think the same as Master. We should prioritise getting out by the shortest route."
"Of course — Tino is leading, right?"
She nodded reflexively at Master's question.
As any Hunter should, she'd already memorised the Treasury's map.
She knew their current position.
No worry of getting lost.
She'd let his guidance find the missing party — but she couldn't keep relying on his kindness forever.
"Of course.
Avoiding every phantom the way Master can might be hard, but..."
"Eh?
Ah — yeah, yeah.
...Well, do try to avoid them.
That's really important."
"Yes.
Of course.
The pain's gone, so I can run properly this time."
"!? R-right.
You were slow earlier.
But — there are rescued people now... don't forget that."
At those words, Tino's cheeks went red with embarrassment.
She'd completely forgotten.
Too aware of being watched by Master, she'd been thinking only of herself.
She'd reflect on this deeply.
Well — the rescued were Hunters of decent level, so they could probably run faster than during her earlier escape — but no need to say that now.
That wasn't what Master meant.
She wanted to find a hole to crawl into, but she breathed deep and pulled herself together.
No time for personal embarrassment now.
From Master's view, there must be plenty of failings in her anyway.
Now she just had to do her best.
Bracing against the urge to shrink under his serious gaze, Tino spoke clearly.
"Also — just to be sure.
With Master here, this might be unnecessary, but... about the human-shaped wolf-knight that took out Rodolphe — I think we'd better hear more."
At a small shadow that slipped in without any footstep, the silver wolf-knight slowly raised its face.
Beneath its foot lay a crushed piece of metal.
A piece of metal carrying a smell it had never caught before.
But the moment it caught it, instinct said: be wary.
Now, however — that individual, possessing intelligence equal to or greater than the Silvermoons of old, knew that piece of metal was safe; that it had been deceived.
And — that the next time they met, it could rip him apart.
The strange chain that had wound around its leg and pinned the silver wolf-knights down had also lost its power and was lying on the ground.
Even if a similar chain were thrown again, it knew how to handle it now.
It wouldn't be a problem.
It lifted its body-length-tall greatsword and turned to the shadow with slow, almost weary motion.
Through the eye-socket of the bone covering the right half of its face, the deep-red eye held an even stronger resentment than before.
The other two — armed with bow, with club, of the same rank — had been waiting in ambush in the room and now lifted their faces too.
What stood at the end of those gazes was — a small human figure with its face entirely hidden by a skull bearing a delighted smile.
It was less than a third the height of the towering silver wolf-knights, yet the aura of death about it greatly surpassed theirs.
Held in its hand was a mid-sized, drawn sword.
Unlike the silver wolf-knights' weapons, the blade was clear as glass.
Stillness Star.
An artefact of clearly different lustre from any ordinary weapon, swayed in a hand that hung casually at its side.
The wolf-knights had no way of knowing that this had once been on the back of a Level-8 Hunter, and had been thrown aside mid-flight.
help.note: 知るよしもない is an idiom: literally "have not even a way of knowing" — the も+ない construction emphasises absolute absence of any path to that knowledge.

help.translation: Its body was clad not in plate mail, but in light gear — like an ordinary human's, made for mobility.
Only the boots, reaching to its knees, shone in silver.
The Treasury 【White Wolves' Den】 is the curse left behind by a history of slaughter — the curse of the magical-beast Silvermoon.
The resentment soaked into the den had a strong influence on the mana-material accumulated there.
What lay in it was hatred toward humans — and at the same time, a strong yearning.
Yearning for power.
Yearning for shape.
Yearning for wisdom.
Yearning and hatred are two faces of the same thing.
That the silver wolf-knights walked on two feet, that they wore equipment, was an expression of that.
And so was the bone — modelled on a human skull — that had appeared to cover half their heads.
Then — what about the individual whose face is hidden by complete bone?
Until now, due to insufficient mana-material, the Treasury hadn't been able to manifest a curse that strong. Now it had become a haunt capable of repelling Level-5 Hunters.
In front of the three silver wolf-knights, the shadow whose face was fully covered by a grinning skull walked steadily forward.
With the hatred they had once carried, the wolf-knights raised their roar.
